stories.headline: Four Denver police officers cleared in fatal October gas station shooting
stories.summary:
District Attorney John Walsh ruled Friday that four Denver police officers were justified in using deadly force during an Oct. 27, 2025, armed robbery at a Maverik convenience store on South Parker Road. Officers Alec Garcia, Vincent Roldan, Brian Martinez and Marco Nunez fatally shot Hosea Rashid Moore, 35, who had taken two female employees hostage at gunpoint. Investigators determined the officers fired 30 rounds at Moore.

Moore entered the store at 8:41 p.m. and held a clerk at gunpoint, telling her to lock the doors, turn off the lights and call police. When officers arrived and forced entry, Moore fired multiple rounds, striking Officer Roldan in the upper left thigh. Roldan applied a tourniquet himself before being transported to Denver Health for surgery.

Walsh said the evidence supported a finding of justifiable force under Colorado law, both in self-defense and defense of others. He credited the officers with potentially saving the lives of the two store clerks. No criminal charges will be filed against any of the four officers.

stories.headline: U.S. Supreme Court strikes down Trump tariffs; Denver business leaders welcome clarity
stories.summary:
The U.S. Supreme Court issued a 6-3 ruling striking down tariffs imposed by the Trump administration under emergency powers law, including the sweeping reciprocal tariffs levied on goods from nearly every country. Within hours, Denver and Colorado business leaders responded by emphasizing the importance of predictability and rule of law in trade policy.

J.J. Ament, president and CEO of the Denver Metro Chamber of Commerce, said the decision provides "important clarity" and helps establish defined lanes for policymakers to craft consistent trade policies. Colorado Chamber of Commerce president Loren Furman said many members across various sectors had faced supply chain disruptions, increased overhead and uncertainty due to the tariffs. Industries affected in Colorado ranged from wheat and bicycles to automobile dealerships.

Despite the ruling, some business leaders cautioned that relief may be temporary. Matthew Groves, president and CEO of one trade-focused organization, said the administration retains multiple avenues to apply tariffs and another attempt is expected. President Trump responded to the decision by announcing a new executive order imposing a 10% global tariff rate above existing tariffs.

stories.headline: Colorado trans youth name-change bill advances after Polis veto threat strips family court provision
stories.summary:
Colorado lawmakers advanced Senate Bill 18 through the Senate Judiciary Committee Wednesday, but only after removing a provision that would have directed family court judges to consider a parent's acceptance of a child's gender identity in parenting time determinations. Sponsors pulled the family court section following a veto threat from Gov. Jared Polis, who said he opposed changes to family law. As amended, the bill would require the automatic suppression of name-change records for minors.

Sen. Katie Wallace, a Longmont Democrat and bill sponsor, said it was painful to remove the provision, which honored the late Sen. Faith Winter, who died in a car crash in November and had championed the same language in prior sessions. Activist Z Williams and Rep. Lorena Garcia confirmed Polis issued the veto threat earlier this month.

Polis' office said LGBTQ advocates and family law attorneys had warned the family court language could harm the very children it was intended to help. However, bill sponsors and advocates disputed that characterization, saying they had successfully negotiated a more modest version of the amendment. Polis' statement stopped short of fully endorsing the bill even without the removed provision, suggesting he would be open to separate record-suppression legislation.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature advances 3-D printed gun ban, domestic violence screening bills
stories.summary:
Colorado Democrats advanced House Bill 1144 this week, which would ban the manufacture, possession and distribution of 3-D printed firearms, key gun components, large-capacity magazines and rapid-fire devices, as well as the instructions to produce them. Supporters say the measure closes gaps left by a 2023 ghost gun law and aligns state law with rapidly evolving technology. Opponents argued the bill is duplicative and could criminalize people unnecessarily.

Two bipartisan domestic violence bills also cleared their first legislative committees. House Bill 1009, the Colorado Mandatory Lethality Assessment Act, would require officers responding to domestic violence calls to ask survivors 11 standardized questions to assess risk of lethality — a practice currently voluntary in most of the state's 64 counties. If a survivor is deemed high risk, officers would be required to immediately connect them with a community-based victim advocate.

A second domestic violence measure would increase interagency coordination when cases involve military service members. The bills respond to a 2025 report from Attorney General Phil Weiser's office documenting a sharp rise in domestic violence incidents and deaths across Colorado. House Majority Leader Monica Duran, a lead sponsor of HB 1009 and domestic violence survivor, said the screening tool could help people escape dangerous situations sooner.

stories.headline: Denver's construction costs rank second-highest in the nation, new industry report finds
stories.summary:
Denver's construction costs are the second-highest among major U.S. markets, driven by luxury projects and complex downtown developments, according to the Beck Group's 2026 construction cost report. The findings underscore longstanding challenges for developers seeking to build affordable or mid-market projects in the Denver metro area.

Colorado has also expanded tax incentives to aerospace, quantum computing and other advanced technologies linked to semiconductor supply chains, a move intended to attract high-tech investment. The incentive expansion reflects state efforts to diversify the economy beyond real estate and tourism.

In a separate business development, law firm WilmerHale leased an additional half floor at 1225 17th St. in downtown Denver at the end of last year, bringing its total footprint at the building to 35,000 square feet. The firm's expansion signals continued demand for Class A office space in the central business district despite broader uncertainty in the commercial real estate market.

stories.headline: $132 million affordable housing development breaks ground in Denver's Globeville neighborhood
stories.summary:
Evergreen Real Estate Group and Globeville Redevelopment Partners broke ground on a $132 million mixed-use development on a city-owned parcel in Denver's Globeville neighborhood, the site of a former car dealership. The project will create housing for families earning between 30% and 80% of the area median income, targeting one of Denver's most underserved communities.

The development represents a significant public-private investment in a neighborhood that has long faced disinvestment and environmental challenges due to its proximity to industrial uses and major highway corridors. City officials have designated Globeville as a priority area for redevelopment and affordable housing expansion.

The project adds to a broader wave of affordable housing construction in the Denver metro area as the city grapples with a persistent shortage of units accessible to lower- and middle-income residents. Details on the total number of units and timeline to completion were not immediately available.

stories.headline: Owners of blighted Edge of Lowry apartments settle Aurora lawsuit, agree to sell properties
stories.summary:
The owners of Aurora's Edge of Lowry apartment complex — a property linked to violent incidents involving Venezuelan gang Tren de Aragua that drew national attention — have agreed to sell the buildings and pay Aurora $300,000 to settle a civil lawsuit brought by the city. The Feb. 10 settlement involves Five Dallas Partners, an affiliate of CBZ Management, and releases the company from city liens and summonses in exchange for the payment and sale.

The company will also hire private security to monitor the five properties on North Dallas Street until they are sold or returned to habitable use. The $300,000 represents a partial reimbursement to the city for costs incurred in responding to, closing and securing the buildings over the past two years. A sixth building at the complex, owned by a separate CBZ-affiliated entity, remains under receivership.

Edge of Lowry gained national attention in August 2024 after residents filmed armed men forcing their way into an apartment, a video that became a focal point in immigration debates during the presidential campaign. Eleven suspected Tren de Aragua members were arrested in December 2024 following the kidnapping and torture of a couple at the complex. CBZ owner Zev Baumgarten has multiple open arrest warrants for failing to appear in Aurora Municipal Court, and the Colorado Attorney General's Office is conducting a separate investigation into the company.

stories.headline: Missing Indigenous Person Alert issued for 13-year-old Denver boy
stories.summary:
The Colorado Bureau of Investigation issued a Missing Indigenous Person Alert Friday for Elias Olivas, a 13-year-old boy last seen in Denver's West Colfax neighborhood at approximately 4:15 p.m. Olivas was last seen near West 13th Avenue and Lowell Boulevard, according to state officials.

Elias is described as 5 feet 11 inches tall, 180 pounds, with brown hair and brown eyes. He is Oglala Sioux and was last seen wearing a black hoodie with a large white cross on the back, blue jeans, black shoes and carrying a white Nike bag.

Anyone with information about his whereabouts is asked to contact the Denver Police Department at 720-913-2000 or call 911.

stories.headline: One person dies following house fire in northwest Aurora
stories.summary:
One person died after a house fire in Aurora's Sable Altura Chambers neighborhood Wednesday afternoon, according to Aurora Fire Rescue. Crews responded to the blaze near East 22nd Avenue and Altura Boulevard at 2:04 p.m. and brought the fire under control within 15 minutes.

A single adult victim was pulled from the structure and transported to a hospital with critical injuries, where the person later died. No other injuries were reported, though five residents of the single-story home were displaced by the fire.

The victim's identity will be released by the Adams County Coroner's Office. The cause of the fire had not been determined as of Thursday, and the investigation remains ongoing.

stories.headline: Denver cannabis lounge fined $10,000 in city's first marijuana hospitality enforcement case
stories.summary:
Tetra Lounge, a cannabis hospitality business at 3039 Walnut St. in Denver, has been fined $10,000 and placed on one year of probation after city inspectors alleged multiple licensing violations during special events on April 20 and July 10 last year. The decision, issued Thursday by Molly Duplechian of the Department of Licensing and Consumer Protection, marks the first licensing discipline case involving a marijuana hospitality business in Denver.

City inspectors alleged that cannabis consumption was occurring inside the building — outside the licensed outdoor patio — and that vendors at both events were unlawfully distributing marijuana. Inspectors also reported the presence of booths selling psilocybin and DMT, which are illegal. Tetra's licensed model allows patrons to bring their own cannabis and pay an entry fee to consume on the outdoor patio, but sales on-site are prohibited.

If Tetra Lounge incurs another violation within the probationary year, it will face an additional $10,000 fine and a 60-day closure. Owner Dewayne Benjamin denied the allegations and said he plans to appeal the decision, adding that media coverage of the enforcement action has already caused drops in event bookings, sponsorships and daily visitors.

stories.headline: Colorado officials clarify Maggie Long murder investigation remains active amid misinformation
stories.summary:
The Colorado Bureau of Investigation issued a statement Friday asserting that the Maggie Long Task Force remains active and is "aggressively pursuing justice" in the December 2017 murder of 17-year-old Maggie Long, whose body was found in the charred remains of her family's Bailey home. Officials said unauthorized flyers containing false information about the case had been circulating, prompting the clarification.

Long was burned alive, and law enforcement has released composite sketches of several suspects over the years, but no arrests have been made. The task force's original website was deactivated in 2021 as part of an administrative change, not as a sign the case had gone cold, officials said.

The investigation involves the CBI, Park County Sheriff's Office, FBI and Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives. A $75,000 reward remains available for information leading to an arrest and conviction. Anonymous tips can be submitted by calling 303-239-4243 or emailing maggie.long.tips@state.co.us.

stories.headline: Avalanche eye trade deadline additions ahead of March 7 deadline
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche returned from the Olympic break Tuesday and resume their season Wednesday in Utah, with the NHL trade deadline now nine days away on March 7. General manager Chris MacFarland is expected to be active in the market, though a repeat of last season's significant deadline moves is unlikely given the team's limited trade assets.

Colorado's primary needs are identified as a bottom-six center who could push Jack Drury to the fourth line and a depth defenseman with size for a playoff run. Logan O'Connor's status for the remainder of the season is also in question, potentially prompting a search for a speedy winger.

Among names mentioned as potential targets are Columbus Blue Jackets centers Boone Jenner and Charlie Coyle, though Columbus is currently pushing for a playoff spot and may not be sellers. Chicago Blackhawks players Ilya Mikheyev and Jason Dickinson, as well as New York Rangers center Sam Carrick, are also listed as lower-cost fits that could address Colorado's depth needs.

comic.teaser: "“Well, according to the dictionary, I’m just a large, flightless bird from Ea..." + 3 more
